pages.full_text:
Eve had a shower. On returning to the van, find Matt & Heather Portal, with Botty dog. They had come to find us. After a cup of tea, we walked back to their lovely house "The Old Cork," next to the church, St. Nicholas, a mile away, past badgers' setts, for a super supper.

__Wednesday__
We arrived back at their house for breakfast at 8.10. Then we set off for St. Peter on the Wall at Bradwell on Sea, (the Fort of Othona.) It was built for St Cedd's mission from Northumberland to the Saxons in 654. (The land round here has the earliest charter in existence of land Transferred by charter from a local chief to the Bishop of London.) The chapel was built in the wall of a Roman Fort. Same age as Sutton Hoo.
After our visit we returned to Matt's house for
pages.notes: Text continues from a previous page (begins mid-sentence 'Eve had a shower'). The right edge of the image shows partial text from the right page (overlap zone): 'Coffe', 'Hal', 'bea', 'Laom', 'We h', 'A Ste' — not transcribed here as they belong to the right page. No handwritten page number visible. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; no year is written. 'Botty dog' appears to be the name/nickname of a pet dog. 'The Old Cork' is the house name. 'Wednesday' is underlined in the original.

pages.full_text:
coffee & left the dogs there. We drove to Hyde Hall. This is quite a modern garden, very beautiful. We looked at the veg & "dry garden" (comparable to Peter Buncombe's "wash gardens."
We had lunch & shopped.
After tea at Matt's house we walked back to
photo_captions: St. Peter on the Wall, Bradwell on Sea  654
pages.notes: Text at top continues from the left page (coffee & left the dogs…). The text then breaks off mid-sentence at the bottom ('we walked back to') — continues on the next spread. A glued-in photograph occupies the lower half of the page showing St. Peter on the Wall chapel; the caption is written vertically alongside the photo: 'St. Peter on the Wall, Bradwell on Sea  654'. The '654' in the caption refers to the foundation date (AD 654), not a page number. 'Hyde Hall' is the RHS garden near Rettendon, Essex. 'Peter Buncombe's "wash gardens"' — 'Buncombe' could also be read as 'Buncombe' or '[?Buncombe]'; reasonably legible. No handwritten page number visible.
